rationale: Authority data for W40K-0081..W40K-0090 — a mid-2010s 40K standalone / series-start cluster spanning multiple registers: Peter Fehervari's debut Fire Caste (2013, jungle-stalemate Commissar Iverson + Colonel Cutler on Phaedra vs Tau, opens the loose 'Dark Coil' weird-40K continuity), Ben Counter's Seventh Retribution (2013, Captain Lysander leading Imperial-Fists 1st Company on civil-war Opis vs a Chaos cult, Iron-Warriors trauma flashbacks), Guy Haley's Eldar-anchored Apocalypse-piece Valedor (2014, Prince Yriel of Iyanden + Biel-Tan + Commorragh Dark Eldar vs converging Hive Fleets Leviathan + Kraken at the Battle of Duriel), Paul Kearney's Dark Hunters: Umbra Sumus (2015, Captain Jonah Kerne + Mortai Company defending the homeworld Phobian and pursuing the Punishers warband to Ras Hanem — series-start), Joe Parrino's box-set tie-in Assassinorum: Execution Force (2015, four-assassin ensemble vs Crimson-Slaughter sorcerer Severin Drask and the Temple of Shades ritual), Sarah Cawkwell's Silver Skulls: Portents (2014, Sergeant Gileas Ur'ten + Prognosticar-driven deployment to Valoria Quintus and a slow-burn Inquisition-manipulation reveal), David Annandale's War-Zone-Fenris tie-in Curse of the Wulfen (2016, Wolf-Lord Harald Deathwolf + the returning 13th-Company Wulfen vs Magnus the Red + Thousand Sons across the Fenris system, Grey-Knights uneasy-truce subplot), David Annandale's Titanicus ensemble Warlord: Fury of the God-Machine (2016, Princeps Krezoc / Pallidus Mor + Princeps Syagrius / Imperial Hunters forced to fight together on a Chaos-overrun forge-world), Paul Kearney's Calgar's Fury (2016, Marneus Calgar leading five Ultramarines companies + Inquisition + Adeptus Mechanicus into the space hulk Fury near Iax, morally murky cooperation), and Ian St. Martin's Lucius: The Faultless Blade (2016, Lucius the Eternal vs World Eaters + Fabius-Bile-aligned Emperor's Children + Dark Eldar, series-start of the Lucius trilogy). Structural notes: no format-conflict or low-confidence flags this iteration (mid-2010s Black-Library entries are uniformly well-documented); first authority-layer entries for Imperial Fists' Lysander, the Dark Hunters chapter (White-Scars-successor), the Silver Skulls chapter (also White-Scars-successor), Officio Assassinorum + its four temple sub-factions, Pallidus Mor + Imperial Hunters Titan Legios, the Crimson Slaughter Chaos warband, the Punishers Chaos warband, and the Emperor's Children legion as primary protagonist faction.

overrides.synopsis: Standalone Imperial-Guard-anchored 40K novel set on the jungle-and-swamp death-world of Phaedra, where the Imperium has been bogged down in a decade-long stalemate against a T'au expansion force backed by local secessionist insurgents. The 19th Arkan Confederates — a fresh regiment still haunted by the trauma of their home world's recent rebellion — are deployed to break the deadlock, but command sends them in piecemeal and the regiment scatters under fire. Dual Imperial POV across the campaign: Commissar Holt Iverson, who has spent so long on Phaedra that his sense of duty has corroded into private vendetta, sees in the Confederates a last chance at redemption; Colonel Ensor Cutler holds the regimental command line as the campaign disintegrates. Phaedra itself functions as an antagonist — a Heart-of-Darkness / Apocalypse-Now register where the warp leaks through the canopy, ghosts of past Imperial atrocities surface, and Tau Empire infiltrators move through the trees. Opens Fehervari's loose 'Dark Coil' continuity: weird-40K standalones that share an undercurrent of cosmic-horror taint without explicit cross-references.

Captain Darnath Lysander leads the Imperial Fists' First Company to the world of Opis, where the planetary defence force has fractured into civil war and the Imperium's grip is slipping. The Seventh Crusade-company response is meant to be a clean intervention, but the rebellion proves to be a front for a Chaos cult deep inside the Opisian aristocracy — and the cult's master reaches back into Lysander's own captivity under Warsmith Shon'tu of the Iron Warriors. Officio Assassinorum operatives stiffen the late push. A grim Imperial Fists chapter-piece on the cost of uncompromising siegecraft.

Apocalypse-scale Eldar novel set at the Battle of Duriel. Prince Yriel of Iyanden — carrying the consequences of an earlier engagement his farseers diverted into the warp — learns that the diverted fragment of Hive Fleet Kraken has re-emerged on a collision course with Hive Fleet Leviathan, and that the two swarms' genetic exchange would produce something the galaxy cannot survive. Yriel forges an uneasy three-way alliance with the Aspect-Warrior swordwind of Biel-Tan and the Dark Eldar of Commorragh, who fight only as long as the soul-toll suits them. Wraithguard, Aspect shrines, Kabal raiders and Tyranid bioforms collide across battlefield set pieces; a somber-cosmic register in which even victory only postpones the long extinction.

Series-start Dark Hunters novel. The chapter — a White-Scars successor whose silent-hunting doctrine descends from the Sahrmatae of homeworld Phobian — repulses a Chaos raid that turns out to be a feint. The real campaign is on Ras Hanem, where the Punishers, a warband the chapter believed destroyed years before, have returned for vengeance and are marauding its hive cities. Captain Jonah Kerne of Mortai Company leads the response: a slow-burning pursuit through fortified districts, joint-arms with PDF and Imperial Guard, and a decapitation strike at the warband's command. A grim chapter-portrait that closes on the motto's literal meaning — we are the shadow.

Box-set tie-in novella for the Officio Assassinorum. Four operatives — a Vindicare sniper, an Eversor berserker, a Callidus shapeshifter and a Culexus pariah — are dispatched as a single Execution Force to stop Severin Drask, a Sorcerer-Lord of the Crimson Slaughter, before he completes a ritual at the Temple of Shades that would unleash a daemonic tide on the Imperium. The four temples are so doctrinally different that their cooperation is the central tension: Eversor psychosis grates against Culexus silence, Callidus shape-shifting collides with Vindicare discipline, and Crimson Slaughter cabalists hunt the team through the temple as the team hunts Drask. A heist-and-last-stand procedural at squad scale, structured around each temple's turn in the spotlight.

Mid-series Silver Skulls novel. The chapter — a White-Scars successor whose deployments are dictated by an order of Prognosticars, prophetic psyker-priests — is sent to Valoria Quintus to crush an insurrection the prophecy promises will fall quickly. The campaign is anchored by Sergeant Gileas Ur'ten, a rogue-element battle-brother whose temper holds him at angles to command but whose squad becomes the spearhead. As the war drags on the prophecy frays: the Inquisition has arrived with its own interest in the Prognosticars themselves, and the chapter begins to look like it is being steered by exactly the powers it thought it was fighting. A slow-burn political-mystery over a war-story spine.

War-Zone-Fenris tie-in. Ten thousand years after Prospero, the Wulfen — the lost 13th Company of the Space Wolves — return in scattered packs across the Fenris system, and the chapter commits its great companies to recover them. Wolf-Lord Harald Deathwolf anchors the response. At first the Wulfen read as the closest sign in millennia that Leman Russ might return, but the warp has marked them deeply, and present-day Space Wolves turn Wulfen on contact. Meanwhile cult activity across the system resolves, seen from orbit, into the glyph of Magnus the Red — the long-prepared revenge of the Thousand Sons. Grey Knights arrive demanding the Wulfen be handed to the Ordo Malleus; an uneasy truce holds the line. A tragic chapter-piece on what was lost at Prospero and what came back.

Adeptus Titanicus dual-POV novel. The Pallidus Mor — a stoic Titan Legion still recovering from a near-disastrous Tyranid engagement — is reassigned to a forge world fallen to Chaos and forced to fight alongside the Imperial Hunters, an aristocratic legion whose princeps treat Pallidus Mor as common-stock and chase glory-kills rather than coordinate. Princeps Ferantha Krezoc of Warlord Gloria Vastator holds the line; Princeps Adrel Syagrius of Warlord Augustus Secutor drives the friction until the Chaos enemy forces unwilling cooperation. City-after-city Titan combat anchors the action; beneath it, an Omnissiah-versus-Emperor cult-axis keeps a slow drumbeat. A god-machine novel where the ego-war between Imperium legios runs louder than the Chaos enemy.

Standalone Ultramarines space-hulk novel. The hulk Fury drifts into Ultramar near Iax, its ancient hull carrying both Chaos taint and pre-Imperial archeo-tech that Mechanicus and Inquisition both want before the hulk is destroyed. Marneus Calgar commits five Ultramarines companies to a reconnaissance-in-force, with Inquisition strike-teams and Mechanicus skitarii embedded under their own command and their own agendas. The operation blurs the Imperium faction lines: chapter duty, Inquisition purification urge, and Mechanicus archeo-tech hunger pull in three directions while the hulk's xenos and Chaos defenders close in. A morally murky chapter-piece on inter-Imperium cooperation, with Calgar growing certain the hulk is itself a trap.

Series-start Lucius novel. Lucius the Eternal — Soulthief, Champion of Slaanesh, doomed to reincarnate in any opponent who kills him without exulting — finds his Emperor's Children warband exhausted after ten millennia of pursuit of new sensation, his retainers turning on each other and his own body breaking down. He reaches out to an estranged brother of the legion, and three pressures converge: a feud with a Khornate World Eaters champion; the cabal of Emperor's Children loyal to Fabius Bile, the legion's flesh-artist primogenitor; and a Dark Eldar Kabal tracking him into the Webway. Servitor-POV intervals let the reader see Lucius from outside his own narcissism. Decay, addiction, and the impossibility of mortal escape for someone who cannot die.
